introductionTexts.title: Drones in music
text: Drones are long sustained notes used in various musical traditions, notably in Indian Classical music, bagpipes and ambient electronic music.
Drones can be used for meditation, improvisation and other stuff

introductionTexts.title: The pitch of a note
text: The pitch of a note, also called its frequency is measured in Hertz, abbreviated Hz. Press the play button and adjust the volume slider to listen to a note with 110Hz.

introductionTexts.title: Creating a harmony
text: Let's add another note to this drone. A perfect fifth is 3/2 the size of our fundamental note. Press play to give it a listen :)

introductionTexts.title: The western 12-tone scale
text: Western classical music is based on a 12-tone scale. These are the notes most often used in western music today.
Play around with the volume sliders to create your own harmonies

introductionTexts.title: Indian Classical music
text: The Tanpura (also referred to as Tambura) is a musical instrument with an intriguing and also fundamental place in today's Indian Classical music. As you may well know, it is not a lead instrument on which a melody is played. Rather it is used as a background instrument to create a drone accompaniment through the repetitive plucking of four open strings.

Below is the Sama Gana scale. The most common Tanpura tuning is sa, SA, SA, pa - meaning the fundament note, two octaves and pa. sa, SA, SA ma and sa SA SA ni are also commonly used depending on what is being played.
Play around with the volume sliders to see how they sound.

introductionTexts.title: Drones
text: Pick a scale/drone system to listen to, improvise over or whatever you can think of I don't know
